text.tspan:
VECT is an active ransomware threat that targets Microsoft Windows environments and is designed to encrypt user data while simultaneously performing information-stealing activities. Analysis of observed samples indicates that VECT follows a double-extortion model, combining file encryption with the theft of sensitive data to increase pressure on victims.
Once executed, VECT enumerates the local system, modifies user and application directories, and encrypts files using a custom extension. Ransom notes are dropped across multiple directories to ensure visibility, and the malware attempts to establish persistence through user-accessible locations. Observed behavior also includes credential and data harvesting

activity, suggesting broader post-compromise objectives beyond encryption alone.
Dynamic and static analysis confirms that VECT employs anti-analysis techniques such as delayed execution and self-deletion to hinder forensic investigation. The malware is capable of interacting with common Windows utilities and modifying application data, including browser-related artifacts, indicating an intent to collect stored user information.
Based on OSINT collection, malware sample analysis, and corroboration from multiple threat intelligence sources, VECT ransomware represents a credible threat to enterprise environments due to its destructive impact, data-theft capabilities, and continued operational activity.
